sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 3 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=34, col=13), LEFT, MOUSE(row=31, col=31).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 49, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Left green inside left dark outline (absorbed) with W center. Right green at white block with c center. Right dark outline still visible. Level not complete despite trying many combinations.
- Plan: Try a new approach - move right green away from white block, then click on the empty white block to see if it triggers something.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 48 actions; a strong score needs about 28 or fewer. You are 1.7x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
After the sequence:
1. Swap: left green gets c center, right green gets W center
2. LEFT: right green moved LEFT from (30-32, 30-32) to (30-30, 30-32)... wait, that doesn't make sense. Let me re-read.
After LEFT:
- Right green: (30-32, 30-32), c center - wait, no. After swap, right green should have W center.
- Actually, looking at the output:
  - `color=N px=8 bound=[[30, 30], [30, 32], [32, 32], [32, 30]]` - right green at (30-32, 30-32)
  - `color=c px=1 bound=[[31, 31]]` - right green center is c
  - `color=N px=8 bound=[[33, 9], [33, 11], [35, 11], [35, 12]]` - wait, this is (33-35, 9-11)
  - `color=W px=1 bound=[[34, 10]]` - left green center is W
Wait, after swap:
- Left green: (33-35, 12-14), W center (was c, now W)
- Right green: (30-32, 30-32), c center (was W, now c)
Hmm, but I clicked on the left green's center (34, 13) to swap. After swap:
- Left green: W center (was c)
- Right green: c center (was W)
Wait, that's the same as before the swap! Before the swap, left green had W center and right green had c center. After clicking on left green's center, the centers swapped: left got c and right got W. But the output shows left has W and right has c.
Actually wait, let me re-read. Before this sequence, the state was:
- Left green: (33-35, 12-14), W center
- Right green: (30-32, 30-32), c center
After swap (clicking left green center):
- Left green: W center (unchanged?)
- Right green: c center (unchanged?)
No, the output shows:
- `color=c px=1 bound=[[31, 31]]` - right green center is c
- `color=W px=1 bound=[[34, 13]]` - left green center is W
So after the swap, the state is the same as before! The swap didn't work? Or maybe the swap worked but the centers were already W and c, so swapping gives c and W... wait, no.
Let me re-read the output more carefully:
After swap:
- `color=c px=1 bound=[[31, 31]]` - right green center is c
- `color=W px=1 bound=[[34, 13]]` - left green center is W
Before swap:
- Left green: W center
- Right green: c center
After swap:
- Left green: W center (same)
- Right green: c center (same)
The swap didn't change anything! But earlier, clicking on the left green's center DID swap the centers. What's different?
Oh wait, I think I see the issue. The left green is at (33-35, 12-14) and is INSIDE the left dark outline (which is absorbed). When the green is inside the dark outline, clicking on its center might not work the same way.
Actually, looking at the output after swap, the left dark outline is NOT in the segmentation (it's absorbed). So the left green is inside the absorbed dark outline. Maybe clicking on the green's center when it's inside an absorbed dark outline doesn't swap?
After LEFT:
- Left green: (33-35, 9-11), W center - moved LEFT from (33-35, 12-14) to (33-35, 9-11)
- Left dark outline: (33-35, 12-14) - reappeared!
- Right green: (30-32, 30-32), c center
So the LEFT action moved the LEFT green (which has W center) to the left, out of the dark outline. The dark outline reappeared.
After clicking white block:
- Left green: (33-35, 9-11), c center (was W, now c)
- Right green: (30-32, 30-32), W center (was c, now W)
So clicking on the white block (31, 31) SWAPPED the centers! The white block acts as a swap button!
Current state:
- Left green: (33-35, 9-11), c center
- Right green: (30-32, 30-32), W center
- Left dark outline: (33-35, 12-14)
- Right dark outline: (26-30, 44-48)
Now the right green has W center, so it can move. Let me try moving it RIGHT to see if it goes through the purple bar or into the right panel.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Clicking on the white block SWAPPED the centers! Left green now c center at (33-35, 9-11), right green W center at (30-32, 30-32). Left dark outline reappeared at (33-35, 12-14). Key discovery: white block is a swap button!
Plan: Now right green has W center and can move. Try moving it RIGHT to see if it can reach the right panel/dark outline.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Current: right green W at (30-32, 30-32), left green c at (33-35, 9-11)
# Right green has W center, can move. Try RIGHT to go through purple bar.
result = action(['RIGHT'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("After RIGHT:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['pixels'] <= 10:
        print(f"  color={n['color']} px={n['pixels']} bound={n['boundary']}")
print("  level_completed:", result.get('level_completed'))

# If that doesn't work, try RIGHT again or UP/DOWN
if not result.get('level_completed'):
    result2 = action(['RIGHT'])
    seg2 = current_frame.segmentation
    print("\nAfter RIGHT again:")
    for n in seg2['nodes']:
        if n['pixels'] <= 10:
            print(f"  color={n['color']} px={n['pixels']} bound={n['boundary']}")
    print("  level_completed:", result2.get('level_completed'))
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
